# QuickFIX/Go [![Build Status](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/workflows/CI/badge.svg)](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/actions) [![GoDoc](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix?status.png)](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) [![Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/badge/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix)](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) Open Source [FIX Protocol](http://www.fixprotocol.org/) library implemented in Go ### Looking for help with `MessageStore` syntax changes? See v0.9.0 release notes [here](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/releases/tag/v0.9.0) ## About

QuickFIX/Go is a FIX Protocol Community implementation for the Go programming language.

  • 100% free and open source with a liberal license
  • Supports FIX versions 4.0 - 5.0SP2
  • Runs on any hardware and operating system supported by Go (1.21+ required)
  • Spec driven run-time message validation
  • Spec driven code generation of type-safe FIX messages, fields, and repeating groups
  • Support for protocol customizations
  • Session state storage options: SQL, MongoDB, On-disk, or In-memory
  • Logging options: File, Screen
  • Failover and High Availability
  • Daily and weekly scheduling of session connections
  • Integrated support for SSL communicaitons
  • Automated unit and acceptance tests
  • Commercial Support available

## Installation With [Go module](https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/Modules) support, simply add the following import ``` import "github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix" ``` to your code, and then `go [build|run|test]` will automatically fetch the necessary dependencies. Otherwise, run the following Go command to install the `quickfix` package: ```sh go get -u github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix ``` ## Getting Started * [QuickFIX User Manual](https://quickfixengine.org/go/documentation/) * [Go API Documentation](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) * See [examples](https://github.com/quickfixgo/examples) for some simple examples of using QuickFIX/Go. ## FIX Messaging Model To send and receive messages, your application will need a few additional packages. QuickFIX/Go maintains separate packages for tags, fields, enums, messages, and message components auto-generated from the FIX 4.0 - FIX5.0SP2 XML specifications- * [Tag](https://github.com/quickfixgo/tag) * [Field](https://github.com/quickfixgo/field) * [Enum](https://github.com/quickfixgo/enum) * [FIX 4.0](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix40) * [FIX 4.1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix41) * [FIX 4.2](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix42) * [FIX 4.3](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix43) * [FIX 4.4](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix44) * [FIX 5.0](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50) * [FIX 5.0 SP1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50sp1) * [FIX 5.0 SP2](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50sp2) * [FIXT 1.1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fixt11) For most FIX applications, these generated resources are sufficient. Custom FIX applications may generate source specific to the FIX spec of that application using the `generate-fix` tool included with QuickFIX/Go. Following installation, `generate-fix` is installed to `$GOPATH/bin/generate-fix`. Run `$GOPATH/bin/generate-fix --help` for usage instructions. ## General Support

Github Discussions

Our Github Discussions Board is free, public, and easily searchable. It’s the preferred method of user support from the QuickFIX/Go team.

Please provide as much detail as you can when asking a question, and include relevant configurations and code snippets.

FIX Protocol

More information about the FIX protocol can be found at the FIX Protocol website.

Bugs and Issues

Bugs and issues can be submitted by anyone through our GitHub repository issues list.

Note: Please do not submit questions or help requests to the issues list. It is for bugs and issues. If you need help, please use the Discussions board as described above and you’ll be able to send your question to the entire community.

GitHub Issues

Please provide sample code, logs, and a description of the problem when the issue is submitted.

We will try to address new issues as quickly as possible, and we welcome contributions for bug fixes and new features!

## Commercial Support

Connamara Systems offers commercial support for developers who are integrating any of the QuickFIX implementations (Go, C++, Java, .NET). The support is offered in 10-hour bundles and grants developers access, via telephone or email, to the team that created QuickFIX/Go, QuickFIX/n, and are maintainers of QuickFIX.

In addition to offering QuickFIX support, Connamara delivers Made-To-Measure Trading Solutions by bridging the gap between buy and build. By using internally developed trading platform components, Connamara delivers the best of off-the-shelf ISV solutions and custom application development. Coupled with Connamara’s unique licensing model, trading firms can get the best of both build and buy.

## Contributing If you wish to work on QuickFIX/Go itself, you will need [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) and [VSCode](https://code.visualstudio.com/download) on your machine. * Clone the repo and open it with VSCode with Docker running * This repo comes with vscode devcontainer configs in `./.devcontainer/` * Click the pop-up to re-open the project in the Dev Container * This opens the project in a docker container pre-configured with everything you need ### Build and Test The default make target runs [go vet](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/tools/cmd/vet) and unit tests. ```sh make ``` If this exits with exit status 0, then everything is working! ### Generated Code Generated code from the FIX40-FIX50SP2 specs are available as separate repos under the [QuickFIX/Go organization](https://github.com/quickfixgo). The source specifications for this generated code is located in `spec/`. Generated code can be identified by the `.generated.go` suffix. Any changes to generated code must be captured by changes to source in `cmd/generate-fix`. After making changes to the code generator source, run the following to re-generate the source ```sh make generate ``` If you are making changes to the generated code, please create Pull Requests for these changes for the affected repos. ### Acceptance Tests QuickFIX/Go has a comprehensive acceptance test suite covering the FIX protocol. These are the same tests used across all QuickFIX implementations. QuickFIX/Go acceptance tests depend on ruby in path, if you are using the dev container, it is already installed To run acceptance tests, ```sh # generate code locally make generate # build acceptance test rig make build-test-srv # run acceptance tests make accept ``` ## Licensing This software is available under the QuickFIX Software License. Please see the [LICENSE.txt](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/blob/main/LICENSE.txt) for the terms specified by the QuickFIX Software License.

## Overview

The `thrdpty/` directory (git-ignored, kept as a read-only design reference per the repo's "Constitution Principle III") contains **two complete reference implementations of the FIX (Financial Information eXchange) protocol messaging engine**, from which the host Rust project `truefix` is clearly being modeled. Neither is a CLI tool per se — they are libraries/engines — but both ship small code-generation CLIs.

---

## 1. Languages / Frameworks

- **`thrdpty/quickfix/`** — **QuickFIX/Go**: Go (module `github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix`, `go 1.23`). 100% Go reimplementation of the C++ QuickFIX engine. Maintained by Connamara.
- **`thrdpty/quickfixj/`** — **QuickFIX/J**: Java (Maven multi-module, `mvnw` wrapper, `pom.xml`). 100% Java reimplementation of C++ QuickFIX. Official QuickFIX/J project. FIX versions 4.0–5.0SP2/FIXT1.1 and FIX Latest.

Both target the FIX protocol (real-time securities transaction messaging standard owned by FIX Protocol Ltd).

---

## 2. Project Structure

### QuickFIX/Go (`thrdpty/quickfix/`)
Flat Go-package layout rooted at repo top level:
- **Core engine files**: `acceptor.go`, `initiator.go`, `session.go` (24KB), `session_factory.go` (16KB), `session_state.go`, `session_id.go`, `session_settings.go`, `settings.go`, `message.go` (18KB), `message_router.go`, `parser.go`, `validation.go` (12KB), `field_map.go`, `field.go`, `tag.go`, `tag_value.go`, `repeating_group.go`, `msg_type.go`, `registry.go`, `errors.go`, `connection.go`, `dialer.go`, `tls.go`, `application.go`, `begin_string.go`, `log.go`, `store.go`, `session_rejects.go`.
- **State machine** (one file per state): `logon_state.go`, `logout_state.go`, `in_session.go`, `resend_state.go`, `pending_timeout.go`, `latent_state.go`, `not_session_time.go`.
- **Typed field values**: `fix_boolean.go`, `fix_bytes.go`, `fix_decimal.go`, `fix_udecimal.go`, `fix_float.go`, `fix_int.go`, `fix_string.go`, `fix_utc_timestamp.go`.
- **Sub-packages (dirs)**: `config/` (configuration), `datadictionary/` (XML spec parsing), `spec/` (FIX40–FIX50SP2 + FIXT11 XML specs), `cmd/generate-fix/` (code-gen CLI), `log/{file,composite,screen,mongo,sql}/`, `store/{memory,file,mongo,sql}/`, `internal/testsuite/`, `_sql/{postgresql,oracle,sqlite3,mysql,mssql}/`, `_test/` + `_test_data/` (acceptance tests + definitions), `.devcontainer/`, `.github/workflows/`.
- Key files: `go.mod`, `go.sum`, `Makefile`, `.golangci.yml`, `README.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `LICENSE`, `doc.go`.

### QuickFIX/J (`thrdpty/quickfixj/`)
Maven multi-module reactor:
- `quickfixj-base`, `quickfixj-core` (main engine, packages `org.quickfixj` + `quickfix` + `quickfix.mina` — built on Apache MINA networking), `quickfixj-messages/` (per-version FIX message artifacts: fix40, fix41, fix42, fix43, fix44, fix50, fix50sp1, fix50sp2, fixlatest, fixt11, all), `quickfixj-dictgenerator/` (XML dict generator CLI), `quickfixj-orchestration/`, `quickfixj-examples/{banzai,ordermatch,executor}/`, `quickfixj-perf-test/`, `quickfixj-stress-test/`, `quickfixj-all/`, `quickfixj-distribution/`.
- Root: `pom.xml`, `mvnw`/`mvnw.cmd`, `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `customising-quickfixj.md`, `SECURITY.md`, `docs/` (incl. `threading-model.md`, `threading-developer-guide.md`), `src/main/puml/` (dependency diagrams).

---

## 3. Implemented Features / Core Logic

**Shared FIX engine capabilities (both):**
- Full FIX session protocol: Logon/Logout, Heartbeats, TestRequest, ResendRequest, SequenceReset, Gap-fill, Reject, sequence-number management.
- **Initiator** (outbound connector) and **Acceptor** (inbound listener) roles.
- State machine driving session lifecycle (QuickFIX/Go has explicit per-state files: `logon_state`, `logout_state`, `in_session`, `resend_state`, `pending_timeout`, `latent_state`, `not_session_time`).
- Spec-driven **runtime message validation** (`validation.go` in Go) and **compile-time type-safe code generation** of messages/fields/enums/repeating groups.
- FIX versions 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.0SP1, 5.0SP2, plus FIXT1.1 transport; QuickFIX/J adds **FIX Latest**.
- Data dictionary parsing (XML) — `datadictionary/` in Go; `quickfixj-dictgenerator` in Java.
- Header/Trailer/Body field maps, repeating groups, components.
- Application callback interface (Go `application.go`; Java `quickfix.Application` with `onCreate/onLogon/onLogout/toAdmin/fromAdmin/toApp/fromApp`). Java adds `MessageCracker` (convention + `@Handler` annotation) and `ApplicationFunctionalAdapter`/`ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter` for lambda-based type-safe handlers.

**QuickFIX/Go-specific:**
- **Failover / High Availability**: multiple alternate `SocketConnectHostN`/`SocketConnectPortN` cycled on failure.
- **Daily & weekly session scheduling**: `StartTime/EndTime/StartDay/EndDay/Weekdays/TimeZone`.
- **Proxy protocol** support (HAProxy PROXY protocol via `go-proxyproto`).
- Typed FIX field wrappers incl. **decimal** (`shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal` for unsigned), UTC timestamps with configurable precision.
- Concurrency primitives (`InChanCapacity`, `sync.WaitGroup` in generator).

**QuickFIX/J-specific:**
- Apache MINA-based networking; **threaded** (`ThreadedSocketInitiator/Acceptor`) vs single-threaded strategies, timer thread, heartbeat mgmt, queue back-pressure (documented in `docs/threading-model.md`).
- OSGi bundle support (`-PskipBundlePlugin` profile toggle).
- Customisable message/component/field artifacts (see `customising-quickfixj.md`) for specialised FIX integrations.
- `DoNotSend`, `RejectLogon`, `FieldNotFound`, `UnsupportedMessageType`, `IncorrectTagValue` exception flow.
- Example apps: `executor` (sell-side order executor), `ordermatch` (matching engine), `banzai` (buy-side GUI trading client).

---

## 4. Key Data Structures / Types

**QuickFIX/Go:** `FieldMap`, `Message`, `Header`/`Trailer`, `Field`, `TagValue`, `Tag`, `RepeatingGroup`, `SessionID` (BeginString+SenderCompID+TargetCompID+SessionQualifier), `SessionSettings`/`Settings`, `MessageRouter`, `DataDictionary`/`MessageDef`/`FieldDef`/`ComponentType`, typed value types (`FIXBoolean`, `FIXString`, `FIXInt`, `FIXFloat`, `FIXDecimal`, `FIXUDecimal`, `FIXBytes`, `FIXUTCTimestamp`), store interfaces (`MessageStore`) and log interfaces (`Log`).

**QuickFIX/J:** `Message`, `MessageCracker`, `SessionID`, `SessionSettings`, `MessageStoreFactory`/`MessageStore`, `LogFactory`/`Log`, `MessageFactory`/`DefaultMessageFactory`, `SocketAcceptor`/`SocketInitiator`/`ThreadedSocket*`, generated `quickfix.field.*`, `quickfix.fixNN.*` message classes; dictgenerator model: `Repository`, `MsgType`, `Message`, `Component`, `ComponentComponent`, `ComponentField`, `MsgTypeField`, `MsgTypeComponent`, `Field`, `Enum`.

---

## 5. CLI Interface / Commands

**QuickFIX/Go — `generate-fix`** (`cmd/generate-fix/generate-fix.go`): a code-generation CLI.
- Usage: `generate-fix [flags] <path to data dictionary> ...`
- Parses FIX XML data dictionaries (`datadictionary.Parse`), then via Go `text/template` (templates in `internal/templates.go`, helpers in `template_helpers.go`, `globals.go`) generates: `tag/tag_numbers.generated.go`, `field/fields.generated.go`, `enum/enums.generated.go`, per-version package `header.generated.go`/`trailer.generated.go`, and per-message `<MsgName>.generated.go`. Auto-pairs FIX50/FIX50SP1/FIX50SP2 with FIXT11. Uses `sync.WaitGroup` for concurrent generation.

**QuickFIX/J — `dictgenerator`** (`quickfixj-dictgenerator/.../Generator.java`, `main`): builds QuickFIX-style XML dictionaries from the FPL 2008/09 repository files.
- Usage: `Generator [repository path] [major number] [minor number]`
- Emits three files: `session.xml` (admin/transport), `application.xml` (app messages), `merged.xml` (for pre-FIX5.0). Produces `<fix>`, `<header>`, `<trailer>`, `<messages>`, `<components>` (incl. repeating `<group>`), `<fields>` (with `<value>` enums), normalising enum descriptions into valid Java identifiers.

Neither project ships an end-user application CLI beyond these generators; both are libraries. QuickFIX/J's `quickfixj-examples` provide runnable demo apps (executor/ordermatch/banzai).

---

## 6. Configuration Handling

**QuickFIX/Go** uses an INI-style settings file with `[DEFAULT]` and `[SESSION]` sections (parsed by `quickfix.ParseSettings`). The full set of config keys (extracted from `config/configuration.go`, 1186 lines) is:
- **Session identity**: `BeginString`, `SenderCompID`, `SenderSubID`, `SenderLocationID`, `TargetCompID`, `TargetSubID`, `TargetLocationID`, `SessionQualifier`, `DefaultApplVerID`.
- **Scheduling**: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `TimeStampPrecision`.
- **Sequence/reset**: `ResetOnLogon`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetSeqTime`.
- **Validation**: `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `CheckLatency`, `MaxLatency`.
- **Connection/network**: `InChanCapacity`, `ReconnectInterval`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTimeout`, `HeartBtInt`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketTimeout`, `SocketAcceptHost`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `HeartBtIntOverride`, `DynamicSessions`, `DynamicQualifier`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`.
- **Proxy**: `ProxyType`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `UseTCPProxy`.
- **TLS/SSL**: `SocketPrivateKeyFile`, `SocketCertificateFile`, `SocketCAFile`, `SocketPrivateKeyBytes`, `SocketCertificateBytes`, `SocketCABytes`, `SocketInsecureSkipVerify`, `SocketServerName`, `SocketMinimumTLSVersion`, `SocketUseSSL`.
- **Logging**: `FileLogPath`, `SQLLogDriver`, `SQLLogDataSourceName`, `SQLLogConnMaxLifetime`, `MongoLogConnection`, `MongoLogDatabase`, `MongoLogReplicaSet`.
- **Persistence/store**: `PersistMessages`, `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `SQLStoreDriver`, `SQLStoreDataSourceName`, `SQLStoreConnMaxLifetime`, `SQLStoreMessagesTableName`, `SQLStoreSessionsTableName`, `MongoStoreConnection`, `MongoStoreDatabase`, `MongoStoreReplicaSet`.

**QuickFIX/J** configuration is documented at `quickfixj-core/src/main/doc/usage/configuration.html` and uses the same `[DEFAULT]`/`[SESSION]` INI model via `SessionSettings` + factories (`FileStoreFactory`, `FileLogFactory`, etc.).

---

## 7. External API / Integration Integrations

- **SQL stores/logs** (Go): drivers via `database/sql` with bundled SQL DDL for **PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQLite3, MySQL, MSSQL** (`_sql/<db>/`); `mattn/go-sqlite3` dependency. Java QFJ supports JDBC stores similarly.
- **MongoDB** (Go): `store/mongo` + `log/mongo` using `go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver` (`MongoStore*`/`MongoLog*` config).
- **File & in-memory** stores (`store/file`, `store/memory`) and **file/screen/composite** logs.
- **TLS/SSL**: full cert/key config (file or in-memory bytes), CA, min TLS version, SNI `SocketServerName`, insecure skip-verify.
- **PROXY protocol** (HAProxy) via `pires/go-proxyproto`.
- **Networking**: `golang.org/x/net`; Java uses **Apache MINA** (NIO).
- **Decimal math**: `shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal`.
- **CI/Dev**: Go uses Docker devcontainer + Makefile + GitHub Actions; Java uses Maven (`mvnw`) + GitHub Actions (Maven CI, CodeQL), Sonatype Central publishing, GitHub Packages for SNAPSHOTs.

---

## 8. Unique / Notable Features

- **Spec-driven code generation** producing type-safe, compile-checked FIX message/field/enum classes (Go `generate-fix`; Java dictgenerator + generated per-version artifacts). Compile-time prevention of invalid field/message combos.
- **Failover & HA** with alternate host/port cycling (Go).
- **Daily + weekly session scheduling** with timezone awareness.
- **Dynamic sessions** (acceptor sessions created on-the-fly without preconfigured SenderCompID/TargetCompID) and `DynamicQualifier`.
- **ResendRequest chunking** (`ResendRequestChunkSize`), `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum` (modern FIX session enhancements).
- **PROXY-protocol** passthrough support.
- **MessageCracker** + `@Handler` annotation + functional adapters (Java) for ergonomic, type-safe message dispatch.
- **OSGi** bundle artifacts (Java) for modular deployments.
- **FIX Latest** support (Java) and orchestration repository integration.
- Configurable **timestamp precision** and typed **decimal** (incl. unsigned) FIX fields.
- Comprehensive **acceptance test suite** shared across all QuickFIX implementations (Go depends on Ruby; definitions in `_test/definitions/`).

---

### Notes / Gaps
I read both READMEs, `doc.go`, `go.mod`, the Go `generate-fix.go` CLI, the Java `dictgenerator/Generator.java` CLI, the Go `config/configuration.go` header + full config-key list, and mapped full directory trees of both projects. I did **not** deep-read every engine source file (e.g. `session.go`, `validation.go`, the Java `quickfixj-core` classes, the example apps, or the FIX XML specs) due to budget constraints, but the structure, features, config surface, CLIs, and integration points are fully characterized. The host `truefix` Rust project is a reimplementation of these two engines; these `thrdpty/` trees serve as its design reference (explicitly excluded from the repo via `.gitignore`).
args.next_steps: For deeper per-file detail if needed: (1) read `thrdpty/quickfix/session.go`, `validation.go`, `session_factory.go`, and `datadictionary/datadictionary.go` for the Go session/validation internals; (2) explore `thrdpty/quickfixj/quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/` (Session, Message, Acceptor/Initiator, MINA transport) and the three example apps in `quickfixj-examples/`; (3) read `thrdpty/quickfixj/customising-quickfixj.md` and `docs/threading-model.md` for Java customization/threading detail; (4) compare against the host `truefix` `crates/` to map which features have Rust equivalents.

- **Proxy**: `ProxyType`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `UseTCPProxy`.
- **TLS/SSL**: `SocketPrivateKeyFile`, `SocketCertificateFile`, `SocketCAFile`, `SocketPrivateKeyBytes`, `SocketCertificateBytes`, `SocketCABytes`, `SocketInsecureSkipVerify`, `SocketServerName`, `SocketMinimumTLSVersion`, `SocketUseSSL`.
- **Logging**: `FileLogPath`, `SQLLogDriver`, `SQLLogDataSourceName`, `SQLLogConnMaxLifetime`, `MongoLogConnection`, `MongoLogDatabase`, `MongoLogReplicaSet`.
- **Persistence/store**: `PersistMessages`, `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `SQLStoreDriver`, `SQLStoreDataSourceName`, `SQLStoreConnMaxLifetime`, `SQLStoreMessagesTableName`, `SQLStoreSessionsTableName`, `MongoStoreConnection`, `MongoStoreDatabase`, `MongoStoreReplicaSet`.

**QuickFIX/J** configuration is documented at `quickfixj-core/src/main/doc/usage/configuration.html` and uses the same `[DEFAULT]`/`[SESSION]` INI model via `SessionSettings` + factories (`FileStoreFactory`, `FileLogFactory`, etc.).

---

## 7. External API / Integration Integrations

- **SQL stores/logs** (Go): drivers via `database/sql` with bundled SQL DDL for **PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQLite3, MySQL, MSSQL** (`_sql/<db>/`); `mattn/go-sqlite3` dependency. Java QFJ supports JDBC stores similarly.
- **MongoDB** (Go): `store/mongo` + `log/mongo` using `go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver` (`MongoStore*`/`MongoLog*` config).
- **File & in-memory** stores (`store/file`, `store/memory`) and **file/screen/composite** logs.
- **TLS/SSL**: full cert/key config (file or in-memory bytes), CA, min TLS version, SNI `SocketServerName`, insecure skip-verify.
- **PROXY protocol** (HAProxy) via `pires/go-proxyproto`.
- **Networking**: `golang.org/x/net`; Java uses **Apache MINA** (NIO).
- **Decimal math**: `shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal`.
- **CI/Dev**: Go uses Docker devcontainer + Makefile + GitHub Actions; Java uses Maven (`mvnw`) + GitHub Actions (Maven CI, CodeQL), Sonatype Central publishing, GitHub Packages for SNAPSHOTs.

---

## 8. Unique / Notable Features

- **Spec-driven code generation** producing type-safe, compile-checked FIX message/field/enum classes (Go `generate-fix`; Java dictgenerator + generated per-version artifacts). Compile-time prevention of invalid field/message combos.
- **Failover & HA** with alternate host/port cycling (Go).
- **Daily + weekly session scheduling** with timezone awareness.
- **Dynamic sessions** (acceptor sessions created on-the-fly without preconfigured SenderCompID/TargetCompID) and `DynamicQualifier`.
- **ResendRequest chunking** (`ResendRequestChunkSize`), `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum` (modern FIX session enhancements).
- **PROXY-protocol** passthrough support.
- **MessageCracker** + `@Handler` annotation + functional adapters (Java) for ergonomic, type-safe message dispatch.
- **OSGi** bundle artifacts (Java) for modular deployments.
- **FIX Latest** support (Java) and orchestration repository integration.
- Configurable **timestamp precision** and typed **decimal** (incl. unsigned) FIX fields.
- Comprehensive **acceptance test suite** shared across all QuickFIX implementations (Go depends on Ruby; definitions in `_test/definitions/`).

---

### Notes / Gaps
I read both READMEs, `doc.go`, `go.mod`, the Go `generate-fix.go` CLI, the Java `dictgenerator/Generator.java` CLI, the Go `config/configuration.go` header + full config-key list, and mapped full directory trees of both projects. I did **not** deep-read every engine source file (e.g. `session.go`, `validation.go`, the Java `quickfixj-core` classes, the example apps, or the FIX XML specs) due to budget constraints, but the structure, features, config surface, CLIs, and integration points are fully characterized. The host `truefix` Rust project is a reimplementation of these two engines; these `thrdpty/` trees serve as its design reference (explicitly excluded from the repo via `.gitignore`).

Next steps:
For deeper per-file detail if needed: (1) read `thrdpty/quickfix/session.go`, `validation.go`, `session_factory.go`, and `datadictionary/datadictionary.go` for the Go session/validation internals; (2) explore `thrdpty/quickfixj/quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/` (Session, Message, Acceptor/Initiator, MINA transport) and the three example apps in `quickfixj-examples/`; (3) read `thrdpty/quickfixj/customising-quickfixj.md` and `docs/threading-model.md` for Java customization/threading detail; (4) compare against the host `truefix` `crates/` to map which features have Rust equivalents.

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

# QuickFIX/Go [![Build Status](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/workflows/CI/badge.svg)](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/actions) [![GoDoc](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix?status.png)](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) [![Go Report Card](https://goreportcard.com/badge/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix)](https://goreportcard.com/report/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) Open Source [FIX Protocol](http://www.fixprotocol.org/) library implemented in Go ### Looking for help with `MessageStore` syntax changes? See v0.9.0 release notes [here](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/releases/tag/v0.9.0) ## About

QuickFIX/Go is a FIX Protocol Community implementation for the Go programming language.

  • 100% free and open source with a liberal license
  • Supports FIX versions 4.0 - 5.0SP2
  • Runs on any hardware and operating system supported by Go (1.21+ required)
  • Spec driven run-time message validation
  • Spec driven code generation of type-safe FIX messages, fields, and repeating groups
  • Support for protocol customizations
  • Session state storage options: SQL, MongoDB, On-disk, or In-memory
  • Logging options: File, Screen
  • Failover and High Availability
  • Daily and weekly scheduling of session connections
  • Integrated support for SSL communicaitons
  • Automated unit and acceptance tests
  • Commercial Support available

## Installation With [Go module](https://github.com/golang/go/wiki/Modules) support, simply add the following import ``` import "github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix" ``` to your code, and then `go [build|run|test]` will automatically fetch the necessary dependencies. Otherwise, run the following Go command to install the `quickfix` package: ```sh go get -u github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix ``` ## Getting Started * [QuickFIX User Manual](https://quickfixengine.org/go/documentation/) * [Go API Documentation](https://godoc.org/github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix) * See [examples](https://github.com/quickfixgo/examples) for some simple examples of using QuickFIX/Go. ## FIX Messaging Model To send and receive messages, your application will need a few additional packages. QuickFIX/Go maintains separate packages for tags, fields, enums, messages, and message components auto-generated from the FIX 4.0 - FIX5.0SP2 XML specifications- * [Tag](https://github.com/quickfixgo/tag) * [Field](https://github.com/quickfixgo/field) * [Enum](https://github.com/quickfixgo/enum) * [FIX 4.0](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix40) * [FIX 4.1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix41) * [FIX 4.2](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix42) * [FIX 4.3](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix43) * [FIX 4.4](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix44) * [FIX 5.0](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50) * [FIX 5.0 SP1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50sp1) * [FIX 5.0 SP2](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fix50sp2) * [FIXT 1.1](https://github.com/quickfixgo/fixt11) For most FIX applications, these generated resources are sufficient. Custom FIX applications may generate source specific to the FIX spec of that application using the `generate-fix` tool included with QuickFIX/Go. Following installation, `generate-fix` is installed to `$GOPATH/bin/generate-fix`. Run `$GOPATH/bin/generate-fix --help` for usage instructions. ## General Support

Github Discussions

Our Github Discussions Board is free, public, and easily searchable. It’s the preferred method of user support from the QuickFIX/Go team.

Please provide as much detail as you can when asking a question, and include relevant configurations and code snippets.

FIX Protocol

More information about the FIX protocol can be found at the FIX Protocol website.

Bugs and Issues

Bugs and issues can be submitted by anyone through our GitHub repository issues list.

Note: Please do not submit questions or help requests to the issues list. It is for bugs and issues. If you need help, please use the Discussions board as described above and you’ll be able to send your question to the entire community.

GitHub Issues

Please provide sample code, logs, and a description of the problem when the issue is submitted.

We will try to address new issues as quickly as possible, and we welcome contributions for bug fixes and new features!

## Commercial Support

Connamara Systems offers commercial support for developers who are integrating any of the QuickFIX implementations (Go, C++, Java, .NET). The support is offered in 10-hour bundles and grants developers access, via telephone or email, to the team that created QuickFIX/Go, QuickFIX/n, and are maintainers of QuickFIX.

In addition to offering QuickFIX support, Connamara delivers Made-To-Measure Trading Solutions by bridging the gap between buy and build. By using internally developed trading platform components, Connamara delivers the best of off-the-shelf ISV solutions and custom application development. Coupled with Connamara’s unique licensing model, trading firms can get the best of both build and buy.

## Contributing If you wish to work on QuickFIX/Go itself, you will need [Docker](https://docs.docker.com/get-docker/) and [VSCode](https://code.visualstudio.com/download) on your machine. * Clone the repo and open it with VSCode with Docker running * This repo comes with vscode devcontainer configs in `./.devcontainer/` * Click the pop-up to re-open the project in the Dev Container * This opens the project in a docker container pre-configured with everything you need ### Build and Test The default make target runs [go vet](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/tools/cmd/vet) and unit tests. ```sh make ``` If this exits with exit status 0, then everything is working! ### Generated Code Generated code from the FIX40-FIX50SP2 specs are available as separate repos under the [QuickFIX/Go organization](https://github.com/quickfixgo). The source specifications for this generated code is located in `spec/`. Generated code can be identified by the `.generated.go` suffix. Any changes to generated code must be captured by changes to source in `cmd/generate-fix`. After making changes to the code generator source, run the following to re-generate the source ```sh make generate ``` If you are making changes to the generated code, please create Pull Requests for these changes for the affected repos. ### Acceptance Tests QuickFIX/Go has a comprehensive acceptance test suite covering the FIX protocol. These are the same tests used across all QuickFIX implementations. QuickFIX/Go acceptance tests depend on ruby in path, if you are using the dev container, it is already installed To run acceptance tests, ```sh # generate code locally make generate # build acceptance test rig make build-test-srv # run acceptance tests make accept ``` ## Licensing This software is available under the QuickFIX Software License. Please see the [LICENSE.txt](https://github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix/blob/main/LICENSE.txt) for the terms specified by the QuickFIX Software License.

[![CodeQL](https://github.com/quickfix-j/quickfixj/actions/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/quickfix-j/quickfixj/actions/workflows/codeql-analysis.yml)
[![Sonatype Central](https://maven-badges.sml.io/sonatype-central/org.quickfixj/quickfixj-core/badge.svg)](https://maven-badges.sml.io/sonatype-central/org.quickfixj/quickfixj-core)

This is the official QuickFIX/J project repository.

## intro
QuickFIX/J is a full featured messaging engine for the FIX protocol (FIX versions 4.0 - 5.0SP2/FIXT1.1 and FIXLatest).
It is a 100% Java open source implementation of the popular C++ QuickFIX engine.

The Financial Information eXchange (FIX) protocol is a messaging standard developed
specifically for the real-time electronic exchange of securities transactions.
FIX is a public-domain specification owned and maintained by FIX Protocol, Ltd (FPL).

For more information see the project website at http://www.quickfixj.org.

## release notes

Check out the wiki: https://github.com/quickfix-j/quickfixj/wiki

## questions
For asking questions please either use the mailing list https://lists.sourceforge.net/lists/listinfo/quickfixj-users or ask on Stack Overflow https://stackoverflow.com/questions/ask?tags=quickfixj .

## issues
Please report issues here: https://github.com/quickfix-j/quickfixj/issues

## security
QuickFIX/J welcomes and appreciates responsible disclosure. Contributors are given appropriate credit in release notes and Git logs.

For security issues in QuickFIX/J itself contact the project maintainer: christoph.john-at-macd.com

For security issues in libraries used by QuickFIX/J contact the relevant project team (e.g. for Apache MINA: https://www.apache.org/security/ ). If you feel they are particularly exploitable via QuickFIX/J also feel free to follow up with the project maintainer as above so that we upgrade to the new version in a timely fashion.

Once a security issue is fixed in QuickFIX/J it will be communicated via the user mailing list and other appropriate channels.

## contributions
Pull requests are always welcome! Best is if you added a unit test to show that a certain bug has been fixed or a new feature works as expected.

## build instructions

Fastest: clone the repo and issue the following command.
```
$ mvnw clean package -Dmaven.javadoc.skip=true -DskipTests -PskipBundlePlugin,minimal-fix-latest
```

Slower: if you only want to skip the acceptance test suite:
```
$ mvnw clean package -Dmaven.javadoc.skip=true -DskipAT=true -PskipBundlePlugin,minimal-fix-latest
```

Slow: if you want to run all tests:
```
$ mvnw clean package -Dmaven.javadoc.skip=true -PskipBundlePlugin,minimal-fix-latest
```
NB: If you want to use the resulting JARs in an OSGi environment you'll have to omit the `-PskipBundlePlugin` option.

## importing the project into the IDE

When the project is first created, it will not have the generated message classes and compile errors will occur! Best is to compile once on the command line before importing the project into the IDE.

If the IDE reports some errors after the compilation with `mvnw clean package`, try to use `mvnw clean install`, like:
```
$ mvnw clean install -Dmaven.javadoc.skip=true -DskipTests -PskipBundlePlugin,minimal-fix-latest
```

## configuration options

https://quickfix-j.github.io/quickfixj/quickfixj-core/src/main/doc/usermanual/usage/configuration.html

This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

toolCalls.description:
This tool executes a given shell command as `bash -c <command>`. Command is executed as a subprocess that leads its own process group.

IMPORTANT:
- This tool is for terminal operations (e.g., git / npm / docker / build & test commands).
- DO NOT use this tool for file operations like reading, writing, editing, or searching files. Prefer the dedicated file tools for those actions.
- Quote any file path that contains spaces with double quotes, e.g. `cd "/path with spaces"` or `python "/path with spaces/script.py"`.
- This tool defaults to non-interactive execution. Set `interactive` to `true` only for a bare REPL such as `python` or `node`, or for a password prompt. Leave it unset for ordinary one-shot commands such as `git status`, `npm test`, `pytest`, or `rg foo`.
- If a command would produce very large output, prefer adding flags/filters (narrow paths, limit counts, request specific fields, machine-readable formats like JSON) to keep output small and unambiguous.
- Very large outputs may be returned as the most recent tail of the output. Earlier content may be truncated from the beginning. If the tool reports a "Full output saved to: <path>", that file contains the full command output. Use the exact path returned by the tool, then prefer searching that file first (grep/rg or platform equivalents) for specific ERROR/WARN/FAIL/SUCCESS markers, then reading small slices (with `read_file` using `offset`/`limit`) or tailing the log file instead of re-running expensive commands.
- Do not append truncating pipelines such as `| tail`, `| Select-Object -Last`, or similar to an expensive first run just to suppress output. Those discard earlier context and force a re-run if you later need omitted lines. Let the tool/log capture the full output, then inspect the saved log file.
- When issuing multiple shell commands in one response, parallelize only when they are truly independent and do not compete for the same files, directories, logs, locks, caches, git state, or ports. If one command depends on another or shared state may race, run them sequentially.

The following information is returned:
- Command: Executed command.
- Directory: Directory where command was executed, or `(root)`.
- Output: Combined command output (stdout/stderr). Can be `(empty)` or an excerpt if very large.
- Error: Error or `(none)` if no error was reported for the subprocess.
- Exit Code: Exit code or `(none)` if terminated by signal.
- Signal: Signal number or `(none)` if no signal was received.

args.result:
## Overview

The `thrdpty/` directory (git-ignored, kept as a read-only design reference per the repo's "Constitution Principle III") contains **two complete reference implementations of the FIX (Financial Information eXchange) protocol messaging engine**, from which the host Rust project `truefix` is clearly being modeled. Neither is a CLI tool per se — they are libraries/engines — but both ship small code-generation CLIs.

---

## 1. Languages / Frameworks

- **`thrdpty/quickfix/`** — **QuickFIX/Go**: Go (module `github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix`, `go 1.23`). 100% Go reimplementation of the C++ QuickFIX engine. Maintained by Connamara.
- **`thrdpty/quickfixj/`** — **QuickFIX/J**: Java (Maven multi-module, `mvnw` wrapper, `pom.xml`). 100% Java reimplementation of C++ QuickFIX. Official QuickFIX/J project. FIX versions 4.0–5.0SP2/FIXT1.1 and FIX Latest.

Both target the FIX protocol (real-time securities transaction messaging standard owned by FIX Protocol Ltd).

---

## 2. Project Structure

### QuickFIX/Go (`thrdpty/quickfix/`)
Flat Go-package layout rooted at repo top level:
- **Core engine files**: `acceptor.go`, `initiator.go`, `session.go` (24KB), `session_factory.go` (16KB), `session_state.go`, `session_id.go`, `session_settings.go`, `settings.go`, `message.go` (18KB), `message_router.go`, `parser.go`, `validation.go` (12KB), `field_map.go`, `field.go`, `tag.go`, `tag_value.go`, `repeating_group.go`, `msg_type.go`, `registry.go`, `errors.go`, `connection.go`, `dialer.go`, `tls.go`, `application.go`, `begin_string.go`, `log.go`, `store.go`, `session_rejects.go`.
- **State machine** (one file per state): `logon_state.go`, `logout_state.go`, `in_session.go`, `resend_state.go`, `pending_timeout.go`, `latent_state.go`, `not_session_time.go`.
- **Typed field values**: `fix_boolean.go`, `fix_bytes.go`, `fix_decimal.go`, `fix_udecimal.go`, `fix_float.go`, `fix_int.go`, `fix_string.go`, `fix_utc_timestamp.go`.
- **Sub-packages (dirs)**: `config/` (configuration), `datadictionary/` (XML spec parsing), `spec/` (FIX40–FIX50SP2 + FIXT11 XML specs), `cmd/generate-fix/` (code-gen CLI), `log/{file,composite,screen,mongo,sql}/`, `store/{memory,file,mongo,sql}/`, `internal/testsuite/`, `_sql/{postgresql,oracle,sqlite3,mysql,mssql}/`, `_test/` + `_test_data/` (acceptance tests + definitions), `.devcontainer/`, `.github/workflows/`.
- Key files: `go.mod`, `go.sum`, `Makefile`, `.golangci.yml`, `README.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `LICENSE`, `doc.go`.

### QuickFIX/J (`thrdpty/quickfixj/`)
Maven multi-module reactor:
- `quickfixj-base`, `quickfixj-core` (main engine, packages `org.quickfixj` + `quickfix` + `quickfix.mina` — built on Apache MINA networking), `quickfixj-messages/` (per-version FIX message artifacts: fix40, fix41, fix42, fix43, fix44, fix50, fix50sp1, fix50sp2, fixlatest, fixt11, all), `quickfixj-dictgenerator/` (XML dict generator CLI), `quickfixj-orchestration/`, `quickfixj-examples/{banzai,ordermatch,executor}/`, `quickfixj-perf-test/`, `quickfixj-stress-test/`, `quickfixj-all/`, `quickfixj-distribution/`.
- Root: `pom.xml`, `mvnw`/`mvnw.cmd`, `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `customising-quickfixj.md`, `SECURITY.md`, `docs/` (incl. `threading-model.md`, `threading-developer-guide.md`), `src/main/puml/` (dependency diagrams).

---

## 3. Implemented Features / Core Logic

**Shared FIX engine capabilities (both):**
- Full FIX session protocol: Logon/Logout, Heartbeats, TestRequest, ResendRequest, SequenceReset, Gap-fill, Reject, sequence-number management.
- **Initiator** (outbound connector) and **Acceptor** (inbound listener) roles.
- State machine driving session lifecycle (QuickFIX/Go has explicit per-state files: `logon_state`, `logout_state`, `in_session`, `resend_state`, `pending_timeout`, `latent_state`, `not_session_time`).
- Spec-driven **runtime message validation** (`validation.go` in Go) and **compile-time type-safe code generation** of messages/fields/enums/repeating groups.
- FIX versions 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.0SP1, 5.0SP2, plus FIXT1.1 transport; QuickFIX/J adds **FIX Latest**.
- Data dictionary parsing (XML) — `datadictionary/` in Go; `quickfixj-dictgenerator` in Java.
- Header/Trailer/Body field maps, repeating groups, components.
- Application callback interface (Go `application.go`; Java `quickfix.Application` with `onCreate/onLogon/onLogout/toAdmin/fromAdmin/toApp/fromApp`). Java adds `MessageCracker` (convention + `@Handler` annotation) and `ApplicationFunctionalAdapter`/`ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter` for lambda-based type-safe handlers.

**QuickFIX/Go-specific:**
- **Failover / High Availability**: multiple alternate `SocketConnectHostN`/`SocketConnectPortN` cycled on failure.
- **Daily & weekly session scheduling**: `StartTime/EndTime/StartDay/EndDay/Weekdays/TimeZone`.
- **Proxy protocol** support (HAProxy PROXY protocol via `go-proxyproto`).
- Typed FIX field wrappers incl. **decimal** (`shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal` for unsigned), UTC timestamps with configurable precision.
- Concurrency primitives (`InChanCapacity`, `sync.WaitGroup` in generator).

**QuickFIX/J-specific:**
- Apache MINA-based networking; **threaded** (`ThreadedSocketInitiator/Acceptor`) vs single-threaded strategies, timer thread, heartbeat mgmt, queue back-pressure (documented in `docs/threading-model.md`).
- OSGi bundle support (`-PskipBundlePlugin` profile toggle).
- Customisable message/component/field artifacts (see `customising-quickfixj.md`) for specialised FIX integrations.
- `DoNotSend`, `RejectLogon`, `FieldNotFound`, `UnsupportedMessageType`, `IncorrectTagValue` exception flow.
- Example apps: `executor` (sell-side order executor), `ordermatch` (matching engine), `banzai` (buy-side GUI trading client).

---

## 4. Key Data Structures / Types

**QuickFIX/Go:** `FieldMap`, `Message`, `Header`/`Trailer`, `Field`, `TagValue`, `Tag`, `RepeatingGroup`, `SessionID` (BeginString+SenderCompID+TargetCompID+SessionQualifier), `SessionSettings`/`Settings`, `MessageRouter`, `DataDictionary`/`MessageDef`/`FieldDef`/`ComponentType`, typed value types (`FIXBoolean`, `FIXString`, `FIXInt`, `FIXFloat`, `FIXDecimal`, `FIXUDecimal`, `FIXBytes`, `FIXUTCTimestamp`), store interfaces (`MessageStore`) and log interfaces (`Log`).

**QuickFIX/J:** `Message`, `MessageCracker`, `SessionID`, `SessionSettings`, `MessageStoreFactory`/`MessageStore`, `LogFactory`/`Log`, `MessageFactory`/`DefaultMessageFactory`, `SocketAcceptor`/`SocketInitiator`/`ThreadedSocket*`, generated `quickfix.field.*`, `quickfix.fixNN.*` message classes; dictgenerator model: `Repository`, `MsgType`, `Message`, `Component`, `ComponentComponent`, `ComponentField`, `MsgTypeField`, `MsgTypeComponent`, `Field`, `Enum`.

---

## 5. CLI Interface / Commands

**QuickFIX/Go — `generate-fix`** (`cmd/generate-fix/generate-fix.go`): a code-generation CLI.
- Usage: `generate-fix [flags] <path to data dictionary> ...`
- Parses FIX XML data dictionaries (`datadictionary.Parse`), then via Go `text/template` (templates in `internal/templates.go`, helpers in `template_helpers.go`, `globals.go`) generates: `tag/tag_numbers.generated.go`, `field/fields.generated.go`, `enum/enums.generated.go`, per-version package `header.generated.go`/`trailer.generated.go`, and per-message `<MsgName>.generated.go`. Auto-pairs FIX50/FIX50SP1/FIX50SP2 with FIXT11. Uses `sync.WaitGroup` for concurrent generation.

**QuickFIX/J — `dictgenerator`** (`quickfixj-dictgenerator/.../Generator.java`, `main`): builds QuickFIX-style XML dictionaries from the FPL 2008/09 repository files.
- Usage: `Generator [repository path] [major number] [minor number]`
- Emits three files: `session.xml` (admin/transport), `application.xml` (app messages), `merged.xml` (for pre-FIX5.0). Produces `<fix>`, `<header>`, `<trailer>`, `<messages>`, `<components>` (incl. repeating `<group>`), `<fields>` (with `<value>` enums), normalising enum descriptions into valid Java identifiers.

Neither project ships an end-user application CLI beyond these generators; both are libraries. QuickFIX/J's `quickfixj-examples` provide runnable demo apps (executor/ordermatch/banzai).

---

## 6. Configuration Handling

**QuickFIX/Go** uses an INI-style settings file with `[DEFAULT]` and `[SESSION]` sections (parsed by `quickfix.ParseSettings`). The full set of config keys (extracted from `config/configuration.go`, 1186 lines) is:
- **Session identity**: `BeginString`, `SenderCompID`, `SenderSubID`, `SenderLocationID`, `TargetCompID`, `TargetSubID`, `TargetLocationID`, `SessionQualifier`, `DefaultApplVerID`.
- **Scheduling**: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `TimeStampPrecision`.
- **Sequence/reset**: `ResetOnLogon`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetSeqTime`.
- **Validation**: `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `CheckLatency`, `MaxLatency`.
- **Connection/network**: `InChanCapacity`, `ReconnectInterval`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTimeout`, `HeartBtInt`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketTimeout`, `SocketAcceptHost`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `HeartBtIntOverride`, `DynamicSessions`, `DynamicQualifier`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`.
- **Proxy**: `ProxyType`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `UseTCPProxy`.
- **TLS/SSL**: `SocketPrivateKeyFile`, `SocketCertificateFile`, `SocketCAFile`, `SocketPrivateKeyBytes`, `SocketCertificateBytes`, `SocketCABytes`, `SocketInsecureSkipVerify`, `SocketServerName`, `SocketMinimumTLSVersion`, `SocketUseSSL`.
- **Logging**: `FileLogPath`, `SQLLogDriver`, `SQLLogDataSourceName`, `SQLLogConnMaxLifetime`, `MongoLogConnection`, `MongoLogDatabase`, `MongoLogReplicaSet`.
- **Persistence/store**: `PersistMessages`, `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `SQLStoreDriver`, `SQLStoreDataSourceName`, `SQLStoreConnMaxLifetime`, `SQLStoreMessagesTableName`, `SQLStoreSessionsTableName`, `MongoStoreConnection`, `MongoStoreDatabase`, `MongoStoreReplicaSet`.

**QuickFIX/J** configuration is documented at `quickfixj-core/src/main/doc/usage/configuration.html` and uses the same `[DEFAULT]`/`[SESSION]` INI model via `SessionSettings` + factories (`FileStoreFactory`, `FileLogFactory`, etc.).

---

## 7. External API / Integration Integrations

- **SQL stores/logs** (Go): drivers via `database/sql` with bundled SQL DDL for **PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQLite3, MySQL, MSSQL** (`_sql/<db>/`); `mattn/go-sqlite3` dependency. Java QFJ supports JDBC stores similarly.
- **MongoDB** (Go): `store/mongo` + `log/mongo` using `go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver` (`MongoStore*`/`MongoLog*` config).
- **File & in-memory** stores (`store/file`, `store/memory`) and **file/screen/composite** logs.
- **TLS/SSL**: full cert/key config (file or in-memory bytes), CA, min TLS version, SNI `SocketServerName`, insecure skip-verify.
- **PROXY protocol** (HAProxy) via `pires/go-proxyproto`.
- **Networking**: `golang.org/x/net`; Java uses **Apache MINA** (NIO).
- **Decimal math**: `shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal`.
- **CI/Dev**: Go uses Docker devcontainer + Makefile + GitHub Actions; Java uses Maven (`mvnw`) + GitHub Actions (Maven CI, CodeQL), Sonatype Central publishing, GitHub Packages for SNAPSHOTs.

---

## 8. Unique / Notable Features

- **Spec-driven code generation** producing type-safe, compile-checked FIX message/field/enum classes (Go `generate-fix`; Java dictgenerator + generated per-version artifacts). Compile-time prevention of invalid field/message combos.
- **Failover & HA** with alternate host/port cycling (Go).
- **Daily + weekly session scheduling** with timezone awareness.
- **Dynamic sessions** (acceptor sessions created on-the-fly without preconfigured SenderCompID/TargetCompID) and `DynamicQualifier`.
- **ResendRequest chunking** (`ResendRequestChunkSize`), `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum` (modern FIX session enhancements).
- **PROXY-protocol** passthrough support.
- **MessageCracker** + `@Handler` annotation + functional adapters (Java) for ergonomic, type-safe message dispatch.
- **OSGi** bundle artifacts (Java) for modular deployments.
- **FIX Latest** support (Java) and orchestration repository integration.
- Configurable **timestamp precision** and typed **decimal** (incl. unsigned) FIX fields.
- Comprehensive **acceptance test suite** shared across all QuickFIX implementations (Go depends on Ruby; definitions in `_test/definitions/`).

---

### Notes / Gaps
I read both READMEs, `doc.go`, `go.mod`, the Go `generate-fix.go` CLI, the Java `dictgenerator/Generator.java` CLI, the Go `config/configuration.go` header + full config-key list, and mapped full directory trees of both projects. I did **not** deep-read every engine source file (e.g. `session.go`, `validation.go`, the Java `quickfixj-core` classes, the example apps, or the FIX XML specs) due to budget constraints, but the structure, features, config surface, CLIs, and integration points are fully characterized. The host `truefix` Rust project is a reimplementation of these two engines; these `thrdpty/` trees serve as its design reference (explicitly excluded from the repo via `.gitignore`).
args.next_steps: For deeper per-file detail if needed: (1) read `thrdpty/quickfix/session.go`, `validation.go`, `session_factory.go`, and `datadictionary/datadictionary.go` for the Go session/validation internals; (2) explore `thrdpty/quickfixj/quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/` (Session, Message, Acceptor/Initiator, MINA transport) and the three example apps in `quickfixj-examples/`; (3) read `thrdpty/quickfixj/customising-quickfixj.md` and `docs/threading-model.md` for Java customization/threading detail; (4) compare against the host `truefix` `crates/` to map which features have Rust equivalents.

- **Sub-packages (dirs)**: `config/` (configuration), `datadictionary/` (XML spec parsing), `spec/` (FIX40–FIX50SP2 + FIXT11 XML specs), `cmd/generate-fix/` (code-gen CLI), `log/{file,composite,screen,mongo,sql}/`, `store/{memory,file,mongo,sql}/`, `internal/testsuite/`, `_sql/{postgresql,oracle,sqlite3,mysql,mssql}/`, `_test/` + `_test_data/` (acceptance tests + definitions), `.devcontainer/`, `.github/workflows/`.
- Key files: `go.mod`, `go.sum`, `Makefile`, `.golangci.yml`, `README.md`, `CHANGELOG.md`, `LICENSE`, `doc.go`.

### QuickFIX/J (`thrdpty/quickfixj/`)
Maven multi-module reactor:
- `quickfixj-base`, `quickfixj-core` (main engine, packages `org.quickfixj` + `quickfix` + `quickfix.mina` — built on Apache MINA networking), `quickfixj-messages/` (per-version FIX message artifacts: fix40, fix41, fix42, fix43, fix44, fix50, fix50sp1, fix50sp2, fixlatest, fixt11, all), `quickfixj-dictgenerator/` (XML dict generator CLI), `quickfixj-orchestration/`, `quickfixj-examples/{banzai,ordermatch,executor}/`, `quickfixj-perf-test/`, `quickfixj-stress-test/`, `quickfixj-all/`, `quickfixj-distribution/`.
- Root: `pom.xml`, `mvnw`/`mvnw.cmd`, `README.md`, `AGENTS.md`, `customising-quickfixj.md`, `SECURITY.md`, `docs/` (incl. `threading-model.md`, `threading-developer-guide.md`), `src/main/puml/` (dependency diagrams).

---

## 3. Implemented Features / Core Logic

**Shared FIX engine capabilities (both):**
- Full FIX session protocol: Logon/Logout, Heartbeats, TestRequest, ResendRequest, SequenceReset, Gap-fill, Reject, sequence-number management.
- **Initiator** (outbound connector) and **Acceptor** (inbound listener) roles.
- State machine driving session lifecycle (QuickFIX/Go has explicit per-state files: `logon_state`, `logout_state`, `in_session`, `resend_state`, `pending_timeout`, `latent_state`, `not_session_time`).
- Spec-driven **runtime message validation** (`validation.go` in Go) and **compile-time type-safe code generation** of messages/fields/enums/repeating groups.
- FIX versions 4.0, 4.1, 4.2, 4.3, 4.4, 5.0, 5.0SP1, 5.0SP2, plus FIXT1.1 transport; QuickFIX/J adds **FIX Latest**.
- Data dictionary parsing (XML) — `datadictionary/` in Go; `quickfixj-dictgenerator` in Java.
- Header/Trailer/Body field maps, repeating groups, components.
- Application callback interface (Go `application.go`; Java `quickfix.Application` with `onCreate/onLogon/onLogout/toAdmin/fromAdmin/toApp/fromApp`). Java adds `MessageCracker` (convention + `@Handler` annotation) and `ApplicationFunctionalAdapter`/`ApplicationExtendedFunctionalAdapter` for lambda-based type-safe handlers.

**QuickFIX/Go-specific:**
- **Failover / High Availability**: multiple alternate `SocketConnectHostN`/`SocketConnectPortN` cycled on failure.
- **Daily & weekly session scheduling**: `StartTime/EndTime/StartDay/EndDay/Weekdays/TimeZone`.
- **Proxy protocol** support (HAProxy PROXY protocol via `go-proxyproto`).
- Typed FIX field wrappers incl. **decimal** (`shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal` for unsigned), UTC timestamps with configurable precision.
- Concurrency primitives (`InChanCapacity`, `sync.WaitGroup` in generator).

**QuickFIX/J-specific:**
- Apache MINA-based networking; **threaded** (`ThreadedSocketInitiator/Acceptor`) vs single-threaded strategies, timer thread, heartbeat mgmt, queue back-pressure (documented in `docs/threading-model.md`).
- OSGi bundle support (`-PskipBundlePlugin` profile toggle).
- Customisable message/component/field artifacts (see `customising-quickfixj.md`) for specialised FIX integrations.
- `DoNotSend`, `RejectLogon`, `FieldNotFound`, `UnsupportedMessageType`, `IncorrectTagValue` exception flow.
- Example apps: `executor` (sell-side order executor), `ordermatch` (matching engine), `banzai` (buy-side GUI trading client).

---

## 4. Key Data Structures / Types

**QuickFIX/Go:** `FieldMap`, `Message`, `Header`/`Trailer`, `Field`, `TagValue`, `Tag`, `RepeatingGroup`, `SessionID` (BeginString+SenderCompID+TargetCompID+SessionQualifier), `SessionSettings`/`Settings`, `MessageRouter`, `DataDictionary`/`MessageDef`/`FieldDef`/`ComponentType`, typed value types (`FIXBoolean`, `FIXString`, `FIXInt`, `FIXFloat`, `FIXDecimal`, `FIXUDecimal`, `FIXBytes`, `FIXUTCTimestamp`), store interfaces (`MessageStore`) and log interfaces (`Log`).

**QuickFIX/J:** `Message`, `MessageCracker`, `SessionID`, `SessionSettings`, `MessageStoreFactory`/`MessageStore`, `LogFactory`/`Log`, `MessageFactory`/`DefaultMessageFactory`, `SocketAcceptor`/`SocketInitiator`/`ThreadedSocket*`, generated `quickfix.field.*`, `quickfix.fixNN.*` message classes; dictgenerator model: `Repository`, `MsgType`, `Message`, `Component`, `ComponentComponent`, `ComponentField`, `MsgTypeField`, `MsgTypeComponent`, `Field`, `Enum`.

---

## 5. CLI Interface / Commands

**QuickFIX/Go — `generate-fix`** (`cmd/generate-fix/generate-fix.go`): a code-generation CLI.
- Usage: `generate-fix [flags] <path to data dictionary> ...`
- Parses FIX XML data dictionaries (`datadictionary.Parse`), then via Go `text/template` (templates in `internal/templates.go`, helpers in `template_helpers.go`, `globals.go`) generates: `tag/tag_numbers.generated.go`, `field/fields.generated.go`, `enum/enums.generated.go`, per-version package `header.generated.go`/`trailer.generated.go`, and per-message `<MsgName>.generated.go`. Auto-pairs FIX50/FIX50SP1/FIX50SP2 with FIXT11. Uses `sync.WaitGroup` for concurrent generation.

**QuickFIX/J — `dictgenerator`** (`quickfixj-dictgenerator/.../Generator.java`, `main`): builds QuickFIX-style XML dictionaries from the FPL 2008/09 repository files.
- Usage: `Generator [repository path] [major number] [minor number]`
- Emits three files: `session.xml` (admin/transport), `application.xml` (app messages), `merged.xml` (for pre-FIX5.0). Produces `<fix>`, `<header>`, `<trailer>`, `<messages>`, `<components>` (incl. repeating `<group>`), `<fields>` (with `<value>` enums), normalising enum descriptions into valid Java identifiers.

Neither project ships an end-user application CLI beyond these generators; both are libraries. QuickFIX/J's `quickfixj-examples` provide runnable demo apps (executor/ordermatch/banzai).

---

## 6. Configuration Handling

**QuickFIX/Go** uses an INI-style settings file with `[DEFAULT]` and `[SESSION]` sections (parsed by `quickfix.ParseSettings`). The full set of config keys (extracted from `config/configuration.go`, 1186 lines) is:
- **Session identity**: `BeginString`, `SenderCompID`, `SenderSubID`, `SenderLocationID`, `TargetCompID`, `TargetSubID`, `TargetLocationID`, `SessionQualifier`, `DefaultApplVerID`.
- **Scheduling**: `StartTime`, `EndTime`, `StartDay`, `EndDay`, `Weekdays`, `TimeZone`, `TimeStampPrecision`.
- **Sequence/reset**: `ResetOnLogon`, `RefreshOnLogon`, `ResetOnLogout`, `ResetOnDisconnect`, `ResetSeqTime`.
- **Validation**: `DataDictionary`, `TransportDataDictionary`, `AppDataDictionary`, `RejectInvalidMessage`, `AllowUnknownMsgFields`, `ValidateUserDefinedFields`, `ValidateFieldsOutOfOrder`, `ValidateFieldsHaveValues`, `CheckLatency`, `MaxLatency`.
- **Connection/network**: `InChanCapacity`, `ReconnectInterval`, `LogoutTimeout`, `LogonTimeout`, `HeartBtInt`, `SocketConnectHost`, `SocketConnectPort`, `SocketTimeout`, `SocketAcceptHost`, `SocketAcceptPort`, `HeartBtIntOverride`, `DynamicSessions`, `DynamicQualifier`, `ResendRequestChunkSize`, `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum`.
- **Proxy**: `ProxyType`, `ProxyHost`, `ProxyPort`, `ProxyUser`, `ProxyPassword`, `UseTCPProxy`.
- **TLS/SSL**: `SocketPrivateKeyFile`, `SocketCertificateFile`, `SocketCAFile`, `SocketPrivateKeyBytes`, `SocketCertificateBytes`, `SocketCABytes`, `SocketInsecureSkipVerify`, `SocketServerName`, `SocketMinimumTLSVersion`, `SocketUseSSL`.
- **Logging**: `FileLogPath`, `SQLLogDriver`, `SQLLogDataSourceName`, `SQLLogConnMaxLifetime`, `MongoLogConnection`, `MongoLogDatabase`, `MongoLogReplicaSet`.
- **Persistence/store**: `PersistMessages`, `FileStorePath`, `FileStoreSync`, `SQLStoreDriver`, `SQLStoreDataSourceName`, `SQLStoreConnMaxLifetime`, `SQLStoreMessagesTableName`, `SQLStoreSessionsTableName`, `MongoStoreConnection`, `MongoStoreDatabase`, `MongoStoreReplicaSet`.

**QuickFIX/J** configuration is documented at `quickfixj-core/src/main/doc/usage/configuration.html` and uses the same `[DEFAULT]`/`[SESSION]` INI model via `SessionSettings` + factories (`FileStoreFactory`, `FileLogFactory`, etc.).

---

## 7. External API / Integration Integrations

- **SQL stores/logs** (Go): drivers via `database/sql` with bundled SQL DDL for **PostgreSQL, Oracle, SQLite3, MySQL, MSSQL** (`_sql/<db>/`); `mattn/go-sqlite3` dependency. Java QFJ supports JDBC stores similarly.
- **MongoDB** (Go): `store/mongo` + `log/mongo` using `go.mongodb.org/mongo-driver` (`MongoStore*`/`MongoLog*` config).
- **File & in-memory** stores (`store/file`, `store/memory`) and **file/screen/composite** logs.
- **TLS/SSL**: full cert/key config (file or in-memory bytes), CA, min TLS version, SNI `SocketServerName`, insecure skip-verify.
- **PROXY protocol** (HAProxy) via `pires/go-proxyproto`.
- **Networking**: `golang.org/x/net`; Java uses **Apache MINA** (NIO).
- **Decimal math**: `shopspring/decimal` + `quagmt/udecimal`.
- **CI/Dev**: Go uses Docker devcontainer + Makefile + GitHub Actions; Java uses Maven (`mvnw`) + GitHub Actions (Maven CI, CodeQL), Sonatype Central publishing, GitHub Packages for SNAPSHOTs.

---

## 8. Unique / Notable Features

- **Spec-driven code generation** producing type-safe, compile-checked FIX message/field/enum classes (Go `generate-fix`; Java dictgenerator + generated per-version artifacts). Compile-time prevention of invalid field/message combos.
- **Failover & HA** with alternate host/port cycling (Go).
- **Daily + weekly session scheduling** with timezone awareness.
- **Dynamic sessions** (acceptor sessions created on-the-fly without preconfigured SenderCompID/TargetCompID) and `DynamicQualifier`.
- **ResendRequest chunking** (`ResendRequestChunkSize`), `EnableLastMsgSeqNumProcessed`, `EnableNextExpectedMsgSeqNum` (modern FIX session enhancements).
- **PROXY-protocol** passthrough support.
- **MessageCracker** + `@Handler` annotation + functional adapters (Java) for ergonomic, type-safe message dispatch.
- **OSGi** bundle artifacts (Java) for modular deployments.
- **FIX Latest** support (Java) and orchestration repository integration.
- Configurable **timestamp precision** and typed **decimal** (incl. unsigned) FIX fields.
- Comprehensive **acceptance test suite** shared across all QuickFIX implementations (Go depends on Ruby; definitions in `_test/definitions/`).

---

### Notes / Gaps
I read both READMEs, `doc.go`, `go.mod`, the Go `generate-fix.go` CLI, the Java `dictgenerator/Generator.java` CLI, the Go `config/configuration.go` header + full config-key list, and mapped full directory trees of both projects. I did **not** deep-read every engine source file (e.g. `session.go`, `validation.go`, the Java `quickfixj-core` classes, the example apps, or the FIX XML specs) due to budget constraints, but the structure, features, config surface, CLIs, and integration points are fully characterized. The host `truefix` Rust project is a reimplementation of these two engines; these `thrdpty/` trees serve as its design reference (explicitly excluded from the repo via `.gitignore`).

Next steps:
For deeper per-file detail if needed: (1) read `thrdpty/quickfix/session.go`, `validation.go`, `session_factory.go`, and `datadictionary/datadictionary.go` for the Go session/validation internals; (2) explore `thrdpty/quickfixj/quickfixj-core/src/main/java/quickfix/` (Session, Message, Acceptor/Initiator, MINA transport) and the three example apps in `quickfixj-examples/`; (3) read `thrdpty/quickfixj/customising-quickfixj.md` and `docs/threading-model.md` for Java customization/threading detail; (4) compare against the host `truefix` `crates/` to map which features have Rust equivalents.

## Overview

The `thrdpty/` directory (git-ignored, kept as a read-only design reference per the repo's "Constitution Principle III") contains **two complete reference implementations of the FIX (Financial Information eXchange) protocol messaging engine**, from which the host Rust project `truefix` is clearly being modeled. Neither is a CLI tool per se — they are libraries/engines — but both ship small code-generation CLIs.

---

## 1. Languages / Frameworks

- **`thrdpty/quickfix/`** — **QuickFIX/Go**: Go (module `github.com/quickfixgo/quickfix`, `go 1.23`). 100% Go reimplementation of the C++ QuickFIX engine. Maintained by Connamara.
- **`thrdpty/quickfixj/`** — **QuickFIX/J**: Java (Maven multi-module, `mvnw` wrapper, `pom.xml`). 100% Java reimplementation of C++ QuickFIX. Official QuickFIX/J project. FIX versions 4.0–5.0SP2/FIXT1.1 and FIX Latest.
